author.name: ib

kept.text:
AI has two modes in drug discovery. Accelerate: moving faster through the existing playbook. Unlock: opening frontiers that weren't possible before.

Excited to announce Profluent is partnering with Eli Lilly, the global pharma powerhouse, to unlock breakthrough medicines for patients.

It's a big deal beyond the numbers ($2.25B + royalties): we’ll get to use our frontier AI models and foundational datasets to design proteins focused on large gene insertion, a therapeutic moonshot.

Proteins govern almost everything in biology. We've built a generalizable AI platform to design all proteins. Onward!

kept.text:
$RETA the mother of all peptides 

Let me explain why this one is different.

Ozempic changed everything. Millions of people, dramatic weight loss, one of the biggest drug launches in history. 

Then Eli Lilly came out with tirzepatide hit two receptors instead of one, worked even better. 

That’s doing $45 billion in sales this year alone.

Retatrutide is what comes after that.
It hits three receptors. The third one is the one nobody else touches (glucagon). And what glucagon does is make your liver burn fat directly. Not just eating less. Actually burning more. 

Your metabolism runs hotter at rest, while you’re sitting, while you’re sleeping. Nothing in this drug class does that.

Phase 2 trial results came out and the weight loss curve was still going down at week 48. It hadn’t flattened. People lost 24% of their body weight and the drug wasn’t done yet.

86% reduction in liver fat. Fatty liver affects 40% of the entire world population and doctors have no real answer for it except “try to lose weight.” Retatrutide fixed it in a clinical trial.

Phase 3 data hasn’t dropped yet. When it does, and if it matches Phase 2, this becomes the highest grossing drug in human history.

$RETA is a $34k mcap token on a 1billion $ narrative that the world hasn’t priced in yet

With desci and biotech ecosystem hitting up this is a no brainer play 

GjLdhBVsFhx7PsDcb6ZJMusKszBK3Xu8KdBFEL1vhood

kept.text:
$NVO $LLY 

Boehringer Ingelheim and Zealand Pharma report strong phase 3 data for obesity drug survodutide.

Patients lost up to 16.6% of body weight over 76 weeks vs. 3.2% for placebo.

The drug targets both GLP-1 and glucagon, a combo approach aimed at boosting weight loss.

Earlier data also showed promising results in MASH (fatty liver disease).

Another entrant trying to catch up with leaders Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly, with full data to be presented in June.

Link to article: https://t.co/fWpEgPq1yx

#stocks #Investing

kept.text:
$NVO $LLY 

Jefferies on survodutide from Zealand Pharma and Boehringer:

Weight loss is “just enough”, and focus now shifts to tolerability.

With efficacy broadly in line with Wegovy, investors are expected to look closely at gastrointestinal side effects and discontinuation rates.

Jefferies says the benchmark for the June data will be Wegovy’s phase 3 STEP 1 trial.

Survodutide is likely to be positioned for patients with both obesity and MASH, where safety profile becomes key.

Around 33% of obese patients are estimated to have MASH.

#stocks #Investing

kept.text:
𝗘𝗹𝗶 𝗟𝗶𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗕𝗲𝘁𝘀 𝗢𝗻 𝗔𝗜-𝗗𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗱 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗲 𝗘𝗱𝗶𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘀: 𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝗪𝗵𝗼 𝗔𝘀𝗸𝗲𝗱 𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗲 𝗕𝗿𝗼𝗸𝗲?

Lilly's deal with Profluent to build AI-designed enzymes that insert entire genes is technically impressive. It is also a perfect illustration of how modern medicine thinks: fix the downstream error, never ask what corrupted the upstream signal.

⬩ CRISPR was sold as precision medicine. Profluent's approach goes further by using large language models trained on protein sequences to design novel recombinases. Elegant engineering. But inserting genes into somatic cells does nothing to address why mitochondrial dysfunction, chronic light disruption, and deuterium overload are degrading genomic fidelity in the first place.

⬩ The business logic is clear: patentable enzymes, recurring treatments, captive patient populations. A therapy that repairs root cause has no repeat customers.

At what point does "precision medicine" become precision symptom management with a bigger price tag?
